sn47.8:1.1: “Suppose that there is a foolish, incompetent, unskillful cook who has presented a king or a king’s minister with various kinds of curry:
sn47.8:1.2: mainly sour, mainly bitter, mainly peppery, mainly sweet, alkaline or non-alkaline, salty or non-salty.
sn47.8:2.1: He doesn’t take note of his master, thinking, ‘Today my master likes this curry, or he reaches out for that curry, or he takes a lot of this curry, or he praises that curry.
sn47.8:2.2: Today my master likes mainly sour curry.…
sn47.8:2.3: Today my master likes mainly bitter curry… mainly peppery curry… mainly sweet curry… alkaline curry… non-alkaline curry… salty curry… Today my master likes non-salty curry, or he reaches out for non-salty curry, or he takes a lot of non-salty curry, or he praises non-salty curry.’
sn47.8:3.1: As a result, he is not rewarded with clothing or wages or gifts.
sn47.8:3.3: Because the foolish, incompetent, unskillful cook doesn’t take note of his own master.
sn47.8:3.4: “In the same way, there is the case where a foolish, incompetent, unskillful monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.8:3.5: As he remains thus focused on the body in & of itself, his mind doesn’t become concentrated, his defilements are not abandoned.
sn47.8:3.6: He doesn’t take note of that fact.
sn47.8:3.7: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
sn47.8:3.9: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.8:3.10: As he remains thus focused on mental qualities in & of themselves, his mind doesn’t become concentrated, his defilements are not abandoned.
sn47.8:3.11: He doesn’t take note of that fact.
sn47.8:4.1: As a result, he is not rewarded with a pleasant abiding here & now, nor with mindfulness & alertness.
sn47.8:4.3: Because the foolish, incompetent, unskillful monk doesn’t take note of his own mind.
sn47.8:5.1: “Now suppose that there is a wise, competent, skillful cook who has presented a king or a king’s minister with various kinds of curry:
sn47.8:5.2: mainly sour, mainly bitter, mainly peppery, mainly sweet, alkaline or non-alkaline, salty or non-salty.
sn47.8:6.1: He takes note of his master, thinking,
sn47.8:6.2: ‘Today my master likes this curry, or he reaches out for that curry, or he takes a lot of this curry or he praises that curry.
sn47.8:6.3: Today my master likes mainly sour curry.… Today my master likes mainly bitter curry… mainly peppery curry… mainly sweet curry… alkaline curry… non-alkaline curry… salty curry… Today my master likes non-salty curry, or he reaches out for non-salty curry, or he takes a lot of non-salty curry, or he praises non-salty curry.’
sn47.8:7.1: As a result, he is rewarded with clothing, wages, & gifts.
sn47.8:7.3: Because the wise, competent, skillful cook takes note of his own master.
sn47.8:7.4: “In the same way, there is the case where a wise, competent, skillful monk remains focused on the body in & of itself…
sn47.8:7.9: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.8:7.10: As he remains thus focused on mental qualities in & of themselves, his mind becomes concentrated, his defilements are abandoned.
sn47.8:7.11: He takes note of that fact.
sn47.8:8.1: As a result, he is rewarded with a pleasant abiding here & now, together with mindfulness & alertness.
sn47.8:8.3: Because the wise, competent, skillful monk takes note of his own mind.”
